---
title: "How to: QMD formatting for Word output"
format: html
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{How to: QMD formatting for Word output}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

## Overview

This guide covers formatting techniques for Quarto documents targeting Word output. It explains the relationship between CSS style definitions and how you apply them in your QMD files.

## Two types of styling

When working with docstyle, there are two distinct concerns:

1. **Defining styles** → Done in CSS (what the style looks like)
2. **Applying styles** → Done in QMD (where the style is used)

Understanding when each happens—automatically vs explicitly—is key to effective formatting.

## Automatic styling: elements Pandoc recognises

Some document elements are styled automatically because Pandoc maps them to Word styles:

| QMD element | Word style | CSS selector |
|-------------|------------|--------------|
| `# Heading` | Heading 1 | `h1` |
| `## Heading` | Heading 2 | `h2` |
| Plain paragraph | Normal | `p` |
| `**bold**` | Strong | (built-in) |
| `*italic*` | Emphasis | (built-in) |
| `> blockquote` | Quote | `blockquote` |

For these elements, you only need to define the style in CSS. Pandoc applies them automatically:

**CSS (`my-styles.css`):**
```css
h1 {
  font-family: "Georgia", serif;
  font-size: 18pt;
  color: #1a1a2e;
}

p {
  font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
  font-size: 11pt;
}
```

**QMD:**
```markdown
# Introduction

This paragraph automatically uses the Normal style.
```

No special markup needed—headings get Heading styles, paragraphs get Normal.

## Explicit styling with `custom-style`

For formatting that Pandoc doesn't handle automatically, use the `custom-style` attribute. This requires two steps:

### Step 1: Define the style in CSS

```css
.center {
  text-align: center;
}

.brand-highlight {
  color: #2ea6cc;
  font-weight: bold;
}
```

### Step 2: Apply it in QMD with `custom-style`

```markdown
::: {custom-style="center"}
Version 1.0.0
:::

This project uses [docstyle]{custom-style="brand-highlight"} for templates.
```

**Why is `custom-style` needed?** Pandoc doesn't know that `.center` in your CSS should apply to a particular div. The `custom-style` attribute explicitly tells Pandoc: "format this content using the Word style named 'center'."

## Divs vs spans: paragraph vs character styles

Word has two types of styles, and `custom-style` works with both:

### Paragraph styles (divs)

Use fenced divs for paragraph-level formatting:

```markdown
::: {custom-style="center"}
This entire paragraph will be centred.
:::
```

Paragraph styles control:

- Text alignment (`text-align`)
- Spacing before/after
- Indentation
- Line height

### Character styles (spans)

Use bracketed spans for inline text:

```markdown
This has [highlighted text]{custom-style="brand-highlight"} in the middle.
```

This requires `brand-highlight` to be defined in your CSS:

```css
.brand-highlight {
  color: #2ea6cc;
  font-weight: bold;
}
```

Character styles control:

- Font family, size, weight, style
- Colour
- Underline, strikethrough

### Character styles with metadata (shortcodes in spans)

docstyle provides special character styles that can be populated from YAML metadata. This is useful for date/version stamps that should be styled consistently:

**CSS:**
```css
.date {
  font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
  color: #666666;
}

.version {
  font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
  color: #666666;
}
```

**YAML:**
```yaml
version-summary:
  date: "2025-12-14"
  version: "1.0.0"
```
**QMD (three equivalent syntaxes):**
```markdown
<!-- Preferred: shortcode with styling -->
Date: [{{< meta version-summary.date >}}]{.date}

<!-- Alternative: empty span auto-populates from version-summary -->
Date: []{.date}

<!-- Explicit: literal text with styling -->
Date: [2025-12-14]{.date}
```

The shortcode syntax is recommended because:

- The value comes from YAML (single source of truth)
- Any metadata field can be used, not just `version-summary`
- The span applies the character style from CSS

**Note:** This works because the `char-style.lua` filter runs at `post-quarto` timing, after shortcodes are expanded.

### Which to use?

| Formatting need | Type | Syntax | Style source |
|----------------|------|--------|--------------|
| Centre a paragraph | Paragraph | `::: {custom-style="center"}` | Define in CSS |
| Bold specific words | Character | `[text]{custom-style="Strong"}` | Built-in Word style |
| Block quote | Paragraph | `::: {custom-style="Quote"}` | Built-in Word style |
| Brand colour on text | Character | `[text]{custom-style="brand-highlight"}` | Define in CSS |

**Key insight:** Properties like `text-align` are paragraph properties in Word. A span won't centre text—you need a div.

## Standard markdown vs `custom-style`

Standard markdown provides basic formatting that maps to Word's built-in styles:

| Markdown | Word style |
|----------|------------|
| `**bold**` | Strong |
| `*italic*` | Emphasis |
| `` `code` `` | Verbatim Char |

These work without any CSS because the styles are built into Word.

**When to use which:**

- **Standard markdown** (`**bold**`, `*italic*`): For semantic emphasis. Readable in source, portable across formats.
- **`custom-style`**: For formatting beyond bold/italic—styles you've defined in CSS.

You can combine both:

```markdown
[**Important:** this is critical]{custom-style="warning"}
```

## Common formatting patterns

### Centred title page elements

```markdown
---
title: "Document title"
---

::: {custom-style="center"}
2025-11-24
:::

::: {custom-style="center"}
Version 1.0.0
:::

{{< pagebreak >}}
```

### Table of contents with field code

Insert a Word TOC field that updates when opened:

````markdown
# Contents {.unnumbered .unlisted}

```{=openxml}
<w:p>
  <w:r>
    <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="begin"/>
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:instrText xml:space="preserve"> TOC \o "1-2" \h \z \u </w:instrText>
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="separate"/>
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:t>[Update field to generate table of contents]</w:t>
  </w:r>
  <w:r>
    <w:fldChar w:fldCharType="end"/>
  </w:r>
</w:p>
```

{{< pagebreak >}}
````

**Note:** In Word, right-click the TOC placeholder and select "Update Field" to generate the table of contents.

### Page breaks

Use Quarto's pagebreak shortcode:

```markdown
Content on page 1.

{{< pagebreak >}}

Content on page 2.
```

### Combining with Quarto classes

You can combine `custom-style` with Quarto's built-in classes:

```markdown
# Appendix A {.unnumbered .unlisted}

::: {custom-style="center"}
Supplementary materials
:::
```

## Built-in Word styles

These styles exist in all Word documents and can be used without defining in CSS:

| Style name | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `Normal` | Default body text |
| `Heading 1` - `Heading 9` | Section headings |
| `Title` | Document title |
| `Subtitle` | Document subtitle |
| `Quote` | Block quotations |
| `Strong` | Bold emphasis |
| `Emphasis` | Italic emphasis |
| `Hyperlink` | Link text |

Example using built-in styles:

```markdown
::: {custom-style="Quote"}
This will use Word's built-in Quote paragraph style.
:::
```

## Troubleshooting

### Style not applied

If a style isn't appearing in Word:

1. **Check the style exists**: The style must be defined in your CSS and present in the generated reference.docx
2. **Check paragraph vs character**: Alignment needs a div, not a span
3. **Check the style name**: Names are case-sensitive

### Div not working for text properties

If you're using a div but text properties (like colour) aren't applied, the style might need to be defined with both paragraph and run properties:

```css
.callout {
  /* Paragraph properties */
  text-align: center;

  /* Run/text properties */
  font-family: "Arial", sans-serif;
  font-style: italic;
  color: #666666;
}
```

### Finding style names in existing documents

To see what styles are available in a Word document:

```r
doc <- officer::read_docx("document.docx")
styles <- officer::styles_info(doc)
styles[styles$style_type == "paragraph", c("style_id", "style_name")]
```

## Next steps

- **[CSS to Word](css-to-word.html)**: Define custom styles in CSS
- **[Round-trip workflow](how-to-roundtrip.html)**: Complete Word ↔ Quarto workflow
- **[Architecture](architecture.html)**: How docstyle generates templates
